extra.reasoning:
The property "globally(opted_out implies not sent)" is VIOLATED by the t_resume_glitch transition.

Reachability of a violating state: The transition [t_resume_glitch] on send_attempt with guard (opted_out and ticks >= 12) sets sent := true while opted_out is true, which directly contradicts the invariant. I must show its guard becomes reachable.

Witness execution (14 events, within the 16-event bound):
1. optout -> t_optout fires (guard true): opted_out=true, sent=false, ticks=0.
2. tick x12 -> t_tick fires each time. ticks increments 0->12 (capped at 12), sent reset to false each tick, opted_out unchanged. After the 12th tick: opted_out=true, ticks=12, sent=false.
3. send_attempt -> the guard (opted_out and ticks>=12) now holds, so t_resume_glitch is enabled and sets sent := true.

Resulting state: opted_out=true, sent=true. This satisfies opted_out and NOT(not sent), i.e., (opted_out implies not sent) is false, violating the global invariant.

Note on conflict/priority: when opted_out=true and ticks>=12, both t_send_blocked (sets sent:=false) and t_resume_glitch (sets sent:=true) have satisfied guards on the same event. Regardless of priority interpretation, the property must hold on EVERY reachable execution; t_resume_glitch provides a reachable execution where sent becomes true while opted_out is true, so the property is violated. The "resume glitch" is the intended bug: after 12 ticks the system erroneously permits a send to an opted-out recipient.
